transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: So Alex, you mentioned your cousin invited you to his wedding, can you tell me a bit more about what's been going on for you since you received the invitation?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content:
Um, well, I guess I've just been... thinking about it a lot. I mean, weddings can be kind of overwhelming, right? There's always so much going on, and I just... I don't know. I feel like if I go, people will be asking me questions, and I really don't want to deal with that. It’s like, what do I even say? 

And then there's my mom. I can't help but think about how she'd probably be critical of me, like she always is. It just makes me anxious, you know? So, I kind of just... ignored the invitation. It felt easier that way.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you've been feeling pretty anxious about the idea of going to the wedding, and that's led you to avoid it. Can you tell me more about what you think your mom's criticism would be, specifically?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content:
I mean, it's hard to say exactly. I guess I just have this feeling that she would... I don't know, look at me and think I haven't changed at all. Like, she might say something about my weight or how I’m not doing enough with my life. It’s just this nagging thought that I’m not good enough for her or anyone, really. 

I try to keep my distance from that kind of negativity, so it’s easier to just not go. I don’t want to put myself in a situation where I feel judged or unwanted. It’s just... safer this way.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you're trying to protect yourself from feeling judged or unworthy, and that's led you to create some distance between yourself and others. Have you always felt this way about your mom's opinions, or is there something specific that's changed for you over time?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content:
I guess it’s always been there, but it feels like it’s gotten worse over time. Growing up, I was always the one who stood out for the wrong reasons, you know? My weight, the bullying... it just made everything harder. And my mom, she never really seemed to understand. I felt like I was constantly trying to earn her approval, but it never felt like enough. 

Now, I just... I don’t want to keep putting myself in that position. It’s like I’ve learned that if I stay away, I can avoid the hurt. But then it just makes me feel even more alone, like I’m stuck in this cycle. I don’t know, maybe I’m just making it worse for myself.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you've been carrying around a lot of pain and self-doubt for a long time, and that's made you feel like you need to protect yourself from getting hurt again. Can you tell me more about what it would take for you to feel like you're worthy of your mom's approval, or like you're good enough for her?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content:
I don’t know... I think about that a lot. It feels like there’s this impossible standard I have to meet, and I just can’t figure out what it is. Maybe if I lost weight or had a better job, or if I was just... different in some way, then maybe she’d see me as someone worthy of her love. 

But deep down, I know that it shouldn’t be like that. I should be enough as I am, right? But it’s hard to shake off those feelings of being unlovable. I guess I just wish I could feel more confident in myself, but every time I try, it feels like I hit a wall. So, I end up just avoiding the whole thing instead. It’s easier to stay in my own bubble.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you're caught between wanting to be loved and accepted by your mom, and feeling like you're not good enough to deserve that. Can you tell me more about what happens when you try to imagine yourself as worthy and lovable, even if it's just for a moment? What does that feel like for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content:
Imagining that... it’s kind of scary, to be honest. There’s this part of me that wants to believe it, but then I just feel this wave of doubt crashing over me. Like, what if I’m just fooling myself? What if I really am unlovable? It’s hard to hold onto that idea of being worthy when I’ve spent so long feeling the opposite.

Sometimes, I can picture it, like a little glimmer of hope, but it quickly fades. It feels almost like a tease, you know? I want to feel that way, but then I think about all the times I’ve felt rejected or not good enough, and it just pulls me back down. It’s frustrating because I know I should be able to feel good about myself, but it’s like there’s this heavy weight that keeps dragging me down.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you're struggling to reconcile the idea of being worthy and lovable with the painful experiences you've had in the past. Can you tell me more about what you think would happen if you were to let go of that weight and just allow yourself to feel worthy and lovable, even if it's just for a moment?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content:
Honestly, it feels really daunting to think about. Letting go of that weight... it’s like I’d be stepping into the unknown. There’s a part of me that thinks it could be freeing, like maybe I could finally breathe and not feel so heavy all the time. But then there’s this fear that comes with it—what if I let go and it doesn’t change anything? What if I still feel the same way, or worse, what if I end up feeling even more vulnerable?

I think I’d be scared of opening myself up to disappointment, or worse, rejection. It’s like I’ve built these walls to protect myself, and if I let them down, I might just end up hurt again. So, I guess I just keep holding onto that weight, even if it feels heavy. It’s familiar, and familiar feels safer, even if it’s not really good for me.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you're caught between the desire for freedom and the fear of vulnerability. Can you tell me more about what you think would happen if you were to take a small step towards letting go of that weight, rather than trying to let go of it all at once?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content:
Taking a small step... I guess that sounds a bit more manageable. It feels less overwhelming, you know? Maybe if I just tried to let go of one little thing at a time, it wouldn’t feel so scary. But then I start to wonder what that step would even look like. 

Like, maybe I could try to reach out to someone in my family, just to see how it feels? But then I think about how they might react, and that fear creeps back in. What if they don’t respond well, or what if I just end up feeling rejected again? 

It’s like I want to believe that taking a small step could lead to something good, but I’m also worried that it might just reinforce those old beliefs that I’m not worth it. I guess I’m just not sure if I’m ready to take that risk, even if it’s just a tiny one.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you're feeling stuck between wanting to take a small step towards change and being held back by your fears. Can you tell me more about what you think would happen if you were to take that small step and it didn't go as planned - what would be the worst that could happen?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content:
If I took that small step and it didn’t go well... I guess the worst part would be feeling that familiar sting of rejection. It’s like, I’d be putting myself out there, and if it didn’t work out, it would just reinforce everything I already believe about myself—that I’m unlovable or undesirable. 

I think I’d feel even more isolated, like I’d just confirmed my worst fears. It’s hard to shake off that feeling of being unwanted, and if I faced that again, it would just make it harder to try again in the future. I’d probably retreat even further into my shell, feeling like I can’t trust anyone or even myself. 

So, it’s like I’m stuck in this cycle of wanting to break free but being terrified of what that freedom might cost me. It’s frustrating because I know I want to feel different, but the fear of being hurt again keeps pulling me back.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you're worried that taking a small step towards change could lead to a sense of further isolation and reinforce your negative self-image. Can you think of a time when you did take a risk and it didn't go as planned, but somehow you were still able to move forward and learn from the experience?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content:
Um, I guess there was this one time when I tried to join a support group. I thought it might help me connect with others who understood what I was going through. I was really nervous going in, but I figured it was worth a shot. 

But when I got there, I felt so out of place. I didn’t really connect with anyone, and I ended up leaving early. It felt like a failure, and I remember thinking, “See? You can’t even do this right.” 

But somehow, even though it didn’t go as I hoped, I learned a bit about myself. I realized that it’s okay to feel uncomfortable and that not every experience will be perfect. It also made me think about what I really want in terms of support. So, even though it hurt at the time, I guess it pushed me to reflect on what I need and how I can keep trying, even if it’s not always easy. 

But still, that fear of trying again is always there, you know? It’s like I want to move forward, but I keep second-guessing myself.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you were able to learn something valuable from that experience, even though it didn't go as planned. Can you think of what might be different for you now, compared to when you were trying to join the support group, that might make it easier for you to take another risk and try something new?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content:
I think, looking back, I’ve had some time to reflect on that experience, and maybe I’ve grown a bit since then. I’ve been trying to understand my feelings better, and I guess I’m starting to recognize that it’s okay to feel vulnerable. I mean, it’s still scary, but I think I’m a little more aware of what I need and what I’m looking for in terms of support. 

I also realize that not every experience has to be perfect or lead to a deep connection right away. Maybe I could approach it with a bit more openness this time, like just being okay with the idea that it might not work out, but that’s part of the process. 

And I guess I’ve been thinking about how isolating it can be to avoid everything. So, maybe that’s a little push to try again, even if it’s just a small step. I still have that fear, but maybe I can remind myself that it’s okay to take risks, even if they don’t always lead to what I hope for. It’s just... a little easier to think about now than it was before.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you're feeling a bit more hopeful and open to the idea of taking risks, even if they don't work out as planned. Can you think of one small step you might take in the next week or so that would feel manageable for you, and that might help you build on this sense of hope and openness?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content:
Yeah, I think I could try reaching out to a friend I haven’t talked to in a while. It’s someone I used to feel comfortable with, and I think just checking in could be a good way to reconnect without too much pressure. I mean, it doesn’t have to be a big deal, just a simple message to see how they’re doing. 

I think that feels manageable. It’s not a huge risk like jumping into a big group or anything, but it’s a step towards opening up a little more. If it goes well, maybe it’ll help me feel a bit more connected, and if it doesn’t, at least I tried. It’s a small step, but it feels like it could lead to something positive, and that’s a nice thought.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you're feeling more confident about taking a small step towards reconnecting with a friend. Can you tell me what you think you'll do if your friend responds positively, and if you feel like you're able to open up a bit more to them?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content:
If my friend responds positively, I think I’d feel a mix of relief and excitement. It would be nice to feel that connection again, and maybe I could share a bit about what’s been going on with me. I mean, I wouldn’t dive into everything right away, but just opening up a little could feel good. 

I think I’d want to see how they’re doing too, and maybe we could talk about some of the things we used to enjoy together. It might help me feel more comfortable sharing my own struggles, especially if they’re being open as well. 

But I guess I’d still have that little voice in my head reminding me to be careful. I’d just have to remind myself that it’s okay to be vulnerable and that this is a safe space with someone I trust. If it goes well, it could be a nice reminder that I’m not as alone as I sometimes feel.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you're feeling hopeful about reconnecting with your friend and potentially opening up to them about your struggles. Can you think of what you might say to yourself if that little voice of caution starts to creep in, to help you stay present and focused on the positive possibilities of this connection?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content:
If that little voice starts creeping in, I think I’d try to remind myself that it’s okay to feel nervous, but that doesn’t mean I shouldn’t reach out. I could tell myself something like, “It’s normal to feel vulnerable, and it’s okay to take this step. This friend cares about you, and they might be going through their own stuff too.” 

I’d want to focus on the idea that this is a chance to connect, and that it doesn’t have to be perfect. I could remind myself that even if it feels uncomfortable, it’s part of the process of building relationships and finding support. 

And I might say, “You’ve been through tough times before, and you’ve come out stronger. This is just one small step, and it’s okay to take it at your own pace.” I think that could help me stay grounded and open to whatever happens.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you're developing a helpful inner dialogue to support yourself in taking small steps towards connection and vulnerability. Can you think of one thing you'll do this week to take care of yourself and celebrate this small step, regardless of how it goes with your friend?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content:
Yeah, I think I’d like to set aside some time for myself to do something I enjoy, like going for a walk or listening to some music I love. It’s important for me to acknowledge that reaching out is a big step, and I want to celebrate that, even if it feels small. 

Maybe I could treat myself to a favorite snack or watch a movie I’ve been wanting to see. Just something that reminds me to take care of myself and recognize that I’m trying to make positive changes. I think it would be nice to create a little space for myself to reflect on the week and how I’m feeling, regardless of what happens with my friend. It’s all part of the journey, and I want to honor that.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you're planning to take care of yourself and celebrate your small step towards connection and vulnerability. That's a great way to acknowledge your efforts and prioritize your well-being. Is there anything else you'd like to talk about or any final thoughts you'd like to share before we wrap up our session today?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content:
I think I just want to say that I appreciate having this space to talk about everything. It feels good to sort through my thoughts and feelings, even if it’s a bit challenging. I’m still a little nervous about reaching out to my friend, but I feel like I have a clearer idea of how to approach it now. 

I guess I just want to remind myself that it’s okay to take things one step at a time. I’m learning that it’s okay to be vulnerable and that I don’t have to carry everything alone. So, thank you for helping me work through this. It feels like I’m starting to see a little light at the end of the tunnel, and that’s a nice feeling.
